message:
perf(server/disk_monitor): call malloc_trim(0) on Linux when memory pressure drops back to Ok

When memory pressure drops from a higher level (Warn/Critical) down
to Ok, long-lived in-process allocators (glibc malloc in particular)
can hold onto a large arena's worth of freed chunks without
returning the pages to the OS. That keeps RSS elevated even after
the workload that caused the pressure is gone, making subsequent
pressure-level reports look worse than reality and eating into the
margin before we'd trigger another reclaim cycle.

crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/disk_monitor.rs
- In `monitor_loop`, right after the existing `tracing::info!`
  "memory pressure level changed" log, add a branch that runs when
  `mem_pressure == mcp_agent_mail_core::memory::MemoryPressure::Ok`
  (i.e. we're returning to healthy).
- On Linux (`#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]`), log
  "memory pressure returned to Ok; triggering malloc_trim" and call
  `unsafe { nix::libc::malloc_trim(0); }`. The safety comment notes
  "malloc_trim(0) is thread-safe and safe to call." — it's
  documented as both by the glibc manpage, and passing 0 asks it
  to release as much free top-of-arena memory as possible back to
  the OS.
- The reclaim only fires on the edge transition (other→Ok), not
  every tick, because `last_memory_pressure` already guards the
  enclosing `if mem_pressure != last_memory_pressure` block. That
  keeps the trim from running on every healthy poll.
- No-op on non-Linux targets; the `#[cfg]` gate means the
  `nix::libc::malloc_trim` symbol reference disappears on
  macOS/BSD/Windows builds.
